era_context: The early 10th century CE continues to be characterized by political fragmentation and state consolidation across Eurasia. The Ottonian dynasty is establishing itself in East Francia. The Islamic world remains divided among competing caliphates and regional dynasties. East Asia is in the midst of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period. Southeast Asia sees the continued development of the Khmer Empire. This is an era of military competition, religious expansion, and the gradual development of feudal and bureaucratic systems.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Frankish sphere (sparse documentation)

events.title: Byzantine Empire under Constantine VII; continued consolidation
events.description: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetus (905–959) was reigning as Byzantine Emperor in 917 CE, though he was still a minor and actual power was held by regents, particularly his mother Zoe Carbonopsina and the patriarch Nicholas Mysticus. Constantine VII's reign (913–959) is known for cultural and intellectual achievements, including the compilation of administrative treatises and the patronage of learning. The Byzantine Empire in this period was consolidating its power in the Mediterranean and the Balkans, recovering from earlier Arab raids and internal instability. The reign saw the development of sophisticated diplomatic and administrative systems.
key_figures: Zoe Carbonopsina (regent, mother of Constantine VII)

sources.name: Constantine VII, De administrando imperio (On the Administration of the Empire)

events.certainty_note: Constantine VII's reign is well-documented in Byzantine sources. The year 917 falls within his reign, which is securely dated to 913–959.
cross_references: Led to cultural flourishing in 10th century

events.title: Later Liang dynasty continues; military instability in northern China
events.description: In 917 CE, the Later Liang dynasty under Emperor Taizong (r. 912–923) continued to rule northern China amid ongoing fragmentation and military competition. The dynasty faced pressure from rival states, particularly the Later Tang. The Later Liang period was characterized by military instability, with power concentrated in the hands of military governors and eunuchs. The Later Liang would fall to the Later Tang in 923, ending the first of the Five Dynasties.
key_figures: Zhu Youzhen / Emperor Taizong of Later Liang (r. 912–923)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Official histories of the Five Dynasties

events.certainty_note: The Later Liang dynasty's existence and general chronology are well-established. The year 917 falls within Emperor Taizong's reign.
cross_references: Part of Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period (907–979)
Led to Later Tang conquest in 923-001

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate fragmentation continues; regional dynasties consolidate
events.description: In 917 CE, the Abbasid Caliphate continued to fragment into numerous regional dynasties and emirates. The Samanids controlled Central Asia and Persia, the Ikhshidids (or their precursors) ruled Egypt and the Levant, and various smaller dynasties controlled North Africa, Spain, and other regions. The Abbasid Caliph in Baghdad retained symbolic religious authority but little temporal power. This fragmentation reflected the decentralization of Islamic political authority and the rise of military slavery systems and regional strongmen. Despite political division, cultural and intellectual flourishing continued.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate is well-documented in Islamic historical sources. By 917, the process was well advanced.
cross_references: Part of broader Abbasid decline (9th–13th centuries)
Contemporary with rise of regional dynasties

events.title: Fatimid Caliphate consolidation in North Africa
events.description: The Fatimid Caliphate, founded in 909 CE, was consolidating its power in North Africa in 917 CE. The Fatimids claimed descent from Fatima, daughter of the Prophet Muhammad, and represented a Shi'ite challenge to the Sunni Abbasid Caliphate. By 917, the Fatimids controlled much of the Maghreb and were beginning to expand their naval power in the Mediterranean. Their establishment of a rival caliphate marked a major schism in the Islamic world.
events.certainty_note: The Fatimid Caliphate's existence and general chronology are well-attested in Islamic historical sources. The year 917 falls within the reign of Abdallah al-Mahdi.
cross_references: Led to expansion into Egypt in 969-001

events.title: Khmer Empire; continued temple construction and hydraulic engineering
events.description: In 917 CE, the Khmer Empire was in a period of cultural and architectural flourishing. The period saw continued construction of temples and hydraulic infrastructure, including reservoirs (barays) that supported intensive agriculture and population growth. The Khmer developed sophisticated irrigation systems and integrated Hindu and Buddhist religious traditions. The period is marked by the development of a distinctive Khmer architectural style that would reach its peak at Angkor Wat in the 12th century.
key_figures: Khmer ruler (identity uncertain due to disputed chronology)
events.certainty_note: Khmer chronology for this period is based on epigraphic evidence and Chinese records, which are not always precise for specific years. The identity of the reigning Khmer ruler in 917 is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of Khmer Empire's classical period (9th–13th centuries)
Led to Angkor Wat construction in 12th century

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed for 917 CE. However, the exact chronology of the Khmer Empire remains disputed, and the identity of the reigning Khmer ruler in 917 is uncertain. The Ikhshidid dynasty is traditionally dated to begin in 935, not 917, so any events attributed to them in 917 are anachronistic or refer to precursor dynasties.
historiographic_note: The year 917 CE is moderately well-documented for Western Europe, the Byzantine Empire, and the Islamic world, with less precise documentation for East Asia and Southeast Asia. Byzantine sources on Constantine VII are abundant and relatively contemporary. Islamic sources are rich but often written centuries after the events. Chinese sources on the Five Dynasties are comprehensive. Southeast Asian history relies heavily on epigraphic evidence, which can be difficult to date precisely. The surviving sources are biased toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Court-sponsored chronicles dominate, potentially obscuring alternative perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Constantine VII's reign led to the development of sophisticated Byzantine diplomatic and administrative systems
Later Liang's continued instability led to its conquest by Later Tang in 923
Abbasid fragmentation was contemporary with the rise of the Buyid dynasty
Fatimid consolidation in North Africa led to their expansion into Egypt
